title: Beyond SAF and RSF: Why Sudan’s War Cannot Be Understood Through Two Actors Alone | African Arguments
gist: The author argues Sudan's war is misframed as a two-actor SAF-vs-RSF struggle; in reality governance has been redistributed to a wide range of sub-state actors - Emergency Response Rooms, traditional authorities, local commanders and SPLM-N administrations - who now provide services, mediate disputes and control trade routes, meaning any peace settlement limited to the two military rivals will be disconnected from how the country actually functions.

insights.claim: Governance in Sudan did not collapse with state institutions but moved to a shifting spectrum of sub-state and non-state actors who now perform roles once associated with the state yet are absent from diplomatic negotiations.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Reframes the conflict analysis: peace deals targeting only SAF/RSF will fail to map onto real authority, a key risk for any reconstruction or investment thesis.
insights.quote: governance did not disappear when state institutions collapsed. It moved elsewhere.

insights.claim: Authority in wartime Sudan is increasingly derived from practical capacity - the ability to provide services, resolve disputes, regulate resource access and ensure local security - rather than formal legal status.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: A reusable framework for assessing who actually holds power in collapsed states, useful for risk mapping in fragmented conflict zones.
insights.quote: governance during war becomes less about formal authority and more about practical capacity.

insights.claim: Emergency Response Rooms emerged across Khartoum, Gezira and Darfur as civilian networks organising food distribution, communal kitchens, medical referrals and water provision after municipal services collapsed.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Identifies the de facto local-government actors that any humanitarian or stabilisation strategy must engage.

insights.claim: Traditional authorities and customary mediation bodies in Darfur and Kordofan have kept markets functioning, enabled trader and aid-convoy movement, and negotiated with armed actors to preserve minimum economic order.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Customary structures, not military maps, determine whether commerce and aid flow in conflict zones.

insights.claim: The war has reshaped Sudan's economic geography, weakening Khartoum's centrality and elevating new trade routes connecting Sudan to Chad, Libya and South Sudan that move livestock, fuel and consumer goods.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Cross-border route control is the new locus of economic power; actors controlling them set resource distribution and prices.
insights.quote: new trade routes connecting Sudan to Chad, Libya, and South Sudan gained increasing importance.

insights.claim: Many local economic networks and commanders operate with high practical autonomy driven by market survival and resource control rather than central military command, despite nominal alignment with SAF or RSF.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Means national-level deals cannot reliably deliver local compliance, complicating any settlement.
insights.locator: While some of these economic networks

insights.claim: Checkpoints, transit fees and informal regulation now perform functions once associated with the central state, governing resource access and movement.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Informal taxation systems are durable governance structures that will outlast any ceasefire.

insights.claim: In SPLM-N-controlled parts of South Kordofan and Blue Nile under Abdelaziz al-Hilu, administrative structures, local courts, humanitarian-access mechanisms and economic regulation operate outside both the central state and the SAF-RSF framework.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a functioning parallel state that any settlement must accommodate or risk renewed fragmentation.
insights.locator: This is particularly visible in territories

insights.claim: A settlement focused exclusively on national military actors may redistribute power at the centre but will remain disconnected from the structures actually managing everyday life across the country.
insights.speaker: Eglal Hamid
insights.so_what: Predicts the fragility of any top-down peace deal, a base case for continued instability.
insights.quote: A settlement focused exclusively on national military actors may succeed in redistributing power at the centre. But it risks remaining disconnected
insights.locator: This creates a fundamental political challenge
